interpretation_summary: The replication followed the preregistered plan very closely: a fresh MTurk sample was analysed with the same framing manipulation (lottery vs gift certificate) and the same willingness-to-pay outcome.  After a number of purely technical code fixes (removing an un-available R package, correcting the working-directory logic, and fixing formula syntax) the analysis script ran successfully in the Docker container.  The preregistered focal test – a Welch two-sample t-test comparing mean WTP across the two frames among participants who passed the comprehension check – produced a non-significant difference (p = .143) and, importantly, the direction of the means was opposite to the original claim (lottery > gift, whereas the original reported gift > lottery).  A robustness test that additionally trimmed extreme WTP values (≤ $20) likewise produced a non-significant, directionally opposite result (p = .129).  Thus, using the preregistered criteria (directional consistency and p < .05), the replication does not reproduce the original framing effect.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: High alignment.  The executed code implemented the exact exclusion rules, variable construction, and independent-samples Welch t-test specified in the preregistration.  No analytic flexibility was introduced; all modifications were confined to making the script run inside the container (package removal, file paths, encoding).

deviations.issue_description: Removal of R package ‘yarrr’ (not available on CRAN binary for the base image); effect size calculated with psych::cohen.d instead.
deviations.impact: Low – produced the same Cohen’s d metric; no influence on the t-test outcome.

deviations.issue_description: Hard-coded working-directory in original script replaced by programmatic path detection and explicit data path.
deviations.impact: None – purely technical so that the script could locate the CSV in the container.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Buyers will report a lower mean willingness-to-pay when the prospect is framed as a risky lottery ticket than when it is framed as a gift certificate.
results_comparison.original_results: Experiment 1 (Yang et al., 2013): Mean WTP Lottery = 5.68 USD, Gift Certificate = 9.52 USD; t ≈ –4.17, η² = .11, p < .001 (direction: Gift > Lottery).
results_comparison.replication_results: Comprehension-passed sample (N=168): Mean WTP Lottery = 10.09 USD, Gift Certificate = 9.24 USD; Welch t(85.0) = –1.48, p = .143, 95 % CI [–0.58, 3.96]; Cohen’s d = 0.22 (direction: Lottery > Gift, n.s.).  Robustness subset (WTP ≤ 20, N=147): Mean Lottery = 8.34, Gift = 6.68; Welch t(139.4) = 1.53, p = .129; Cohen’s d = –0.26 (direction still Lottery > Gift, n.s.).
results_comparison.overall_answer: No – the replication failed to detect a statistically significant framing effect and the observed mean difference was in the opposite direction.

replication_report: Replication not successful: the lottery-vs-gift framing produced a small, non-significant mean difference (d ≈ .22) opposite to the original sizeable effect (d ≈ .66).

failure_handling.suggestions: Provide a fully container-ready R script (no hard-coded paths) and limit dependencies to CRAN packages available for all OSs, or include install_github lines where needed.
Write analysis outputs (descriptive tables, t-test objects) to CSV or RDS files so downstream interpreters do not rely on parsing console text, improving transparency.

notes: Because the replication used only one of the original paper’s four experiments and a later MTurk sample (2020), population or time-based changes could partially explain attenuation.  Nonetheless, the complete reversal of direction and lack of significance suggests the original framing effect may be less robust than reported.  Future work could explore moderating factors (stakes, comprehension stringency) and conduct a meta-analysis across multiple replications.
